titleStmt.title: Introduction to the Site of Mount Fenghuang

div.head: 鳳凰山

figure.head: 鳳凰山西南角遠景。2007年拍攝
Mount Phoenix (Mount Fenghuang) seen afar from the southwest; photograph taken in 2007

div.p: 山東省境内有幾座山，山上刻銘以6世紀佛名爲主，鳳凰山乃其中之一。其他幾座山爲本卷的陶山和本叢書《中國佛教石經·山東省》第一卷的天池山、書院東山、二鼓山、雲翠山和大寨山。此佛名會念及佛陀宇宙時空之浩瀚廣大。
不過，鳳凰山刊刻數條10世紀刻銘獨一無二，是當時名將反抗後梁（907－923）末帝朱瑱（913－923年在位）而地方村民據山爲寨的見證。這一事件也見諸正史。因此，這些刻銘使我們有機會比照草根階層記録的地方史料與官方監修的史書。

div.head: 地理位置
div.p: 鳳凰山坐落於魯中山區山脊的西端，隸屬於寧陽縣，山頂的刻銘位於該縣東北偏東17公里、曲阜市以北23公里以及泰安市西南偏南45公里處（見封二地圖）。其地理位置爲東經116.9716度，北緯35.8001度，相當於UTM 坐標系統第50S 帶，東行497430，北行3961780。
鳳凰山山脈南北長約6公里，東西寬約8公里。最高峰海拔530米，西麓山脚海拔120米。
刻銘位於鳳凰山山脈西南峰頂一處稱大山頂子的臺地，從葛石鎮東北約3公里的劉莊村陡然升起約400米，劉莊村深處山谷西端。俯視大山頂子，其山體呈梨形（），通向山頂的小路只有當地人知道。從山頂放眼望去，東爲鳳凰山山脈主峰，南爲曲阜市周邊平原，北邊有更高的山脊，東南是黄家峪。
相鄰的水牛山刻經址位於西南偏西28公里處。高崖上的水牛山1號刻銘朝向西南偏南方，因此不在鳳凰山頂視綫範圍内。從水牛山上也看不見鳳凰山刻經址。 南方最近的刻經址當數40公里之外現已損毀的尖山銘刻。鳳凰山與尖山刻銘，則在彼此視綫之内。尖山舊存刻銘面向鳳凰山北側。鳳凰山東北方向45公里處則爲徂徠山，而刻銘在山體東南，無法相望。

div.head: 地形與空間構成
div.p: 鳳凰山頂的大山頂子爲橢圓形平臺，南北長80米，東西最寬處爲40米（）。平臺上刊刻的五則佛名自北向南依次排開。北邊第一則刻銘朝東，即鳳凰山1號“大空王佛”（）。
兩則佛名繼之刻於鳳凰山1號以南28米左右一塊平坦岩石之上，僅存最後一個“佛”字（鳳凰山2號和3號；）。 “弥勒佛”（鳳凰山4.1號及包括供養人名的鳳凰山4.2號）在鳳凰山3號往南約24米左右，刻銘同樣平鋪。附近有塊滾落的石塊，可見兩面之一刊刻有“華光佛”（鳳凰山5號）。

紀年爲921年的鳳凰山6號是篇幅最長且最爲重要的歷史題記，位於小道西側一塊圓石的南面（）。刻於同一石的鳳凰山6a號僅一行，朝東，爲鳳凰山6號提供補充信息。 鳳凰山7號刻銘較短，刻於小道同側後方的大石之上，朝向東南。鳳凰山8號則位於小道的東側，與鳳凰山6號一樣朝南。同一塊石頭上還有朝東的鳳凰山9號（）。該石北鄰的石塊即上文提到刻有鳳凰山1號的大石。鳳凰山6號往南4米左右的斜坡，刊刻面朝南方的鳳凰山10號刻銘（）。

figure.head: 鳳凰山大山頂子鳥瞰圖。圖片來源：谷歌地球™
Aerial view of Great Mountain Peak, as provided by Google™Earth, Mount Fenghuang

div.head: 地名
div.p: 相傳此山常棲鳳凰，取其吉祥，稱“鳳凰山”。賴非釋名。此山尚有他名見諸記載，但很難確定具體位置，且説法不一。以下討論方誌所載此山山名及環境。

鳳凰山之名最早見於萬曆年間（1573－1620）的《兗州府誌》（1572和1573年序）。其中僅記“鳳凰山在縣東北”，並無更多具體説明。明萬曆元年（1573）刻本《兗州府誌》第2册，第134頁。但提到另一座“在［寧陽］縣西三十里”明萬曆元年（1573）刻本《兗州府誌》第2册，第133頁。名爲“魏家寨山”的山嶺，描述如下：其山高峻，上有石洞，可容數百人。元季人多避兵在此。明萬曆元年（1573）刻本《兗州府誌》第2册，第133頁。
明嘉靖十二年（1533）刻本《山東通誌》也提及“魏家寨山”，不過其位置“在寧陽縣西二十里”。明嘉靖十二年（1533）刻本《山東通誌》上册，第349頁。
清康熙四十一年（1702）刻本《寧陽縣誌》未記鳳凰山，但記録了九頂山最西峰的“鳳凰嶺”。清康熙四十一年（1702）刻本《寧陽縣誌》第573頁。這段描述似乎與此處地形相符，刻有銘文的山峰確實在一排九座山峰中的最西峰。縣誌還記録了“魏家寨”，其描述同於萬曆版《兗州府誌》，不過位置在縣城以東50里。清康熙四十一年（1702）刻本《寧陽縣誌》第581頁。
清乾隆八年（1743）編纂的《寧陽縣誌》將山寨的位置移至一座名爲“鳳凰山”的山峰。此書引用了前兩部方誌關於山寨的描述，不過作了幾處改動。山寨歸爲寧家，不是魏家；且不再是位於山崖上的一個石洞，而是一塊平地；可容人數也由幾百人增至幾千人，曰：
div.quote: 鳳凰山在縣城五十里（柳溝莊東），上有寧家寨。高峻險絶，鳥道而上，有平地可容數千人。元季人多避兵於此。清乾隆八年（1743）刻本《寧陽縣誌》第65頁。
div.p: 所説“高峻險絶，鳥道而上”，雖生動却不夠具體，可適用於該地區眾多山峰，鎸刻銘文的山頂，卻不可能居住數千人。 不過，此縣誌有關山石記録有新的内容：
div.quote: 山石可磨，遠近利之。清乾隆八年（1743）刻本《寧陽縣誌》第65頁。
div.p:
清咸豐二年（1852）刻本《寧陽縣誌》的主修者是知縣陳紀勛（活動於19世紀中葉）。《中國佛教石經·山東省》第四卷（泰山卷）還會提及他，清光緒七年（1875）他在泰山經石峪留下一條題記。他編纂縣誌時做了充分的研究。關於鳳凰山，他主要沿用了乾隆版縣誌，不過詳細描述了該地區的山峰：東爲茅山，傍即大嵎口；南左爲薯山，右爲狼山；東南爲龍山。清咸豐二年（1852）刻本《寧陽縣誌》第6b頁。
根據此地形描述，南方右邊的狼山似爲刊刻佛名的大山頂子。此推測可從丁昭在其2003年出版的《明清寧陽縣誌匯釋》得到證實：“狼山：黄家峪西之山。”（）丁昭 第83頁，註15。
陳紀勛還引述一種版本不詳的《山東通誌》所記甑山：九頂山又名九仙山，即甑山也。清咸豐二年（1852）刻本《寧陽縣誌》第6b頁。 刻於10世紀的鳳凰山6號刻銘有“甑”字，這可證甑山之名在當時已通。
清光緒五年（1879）刻本《寧陽縣誌》僅轉録了咸豐版《寧陽縣誌》，未增加新内容。清光緒五年（1879）刻本《寧陽縣誌》第39a頁。今人常引用該縣誌所記有刻銘的山峰，而忽視了上述定名之難。
1994年出版的《寧陽縣誌》仍將此山稱作鳳凰山，1994年版《寧陽縣誌》第90頁。但1998年在該山所建森林公園則更改了原來歷史悠久、富於詩意的名字，更名爲更悦人的神童山。新規劃的神童山範圍包括刊刻佛名的山峰及其周邊。
不過，山東石刻藝術博物館在2015年最新發表的著作中，仍然沿用此山舊名。賴非第71－73頁。 賴非第49－50頁。因此，本書中也用鳳凰山一名。

div.head: 相關寺院
div.p: 負責刊刻佛名刻銘的寺院不詳。縣誌所載寺院中，最早且最靠近刻銘處者爲石碣寺，原名雲蓋寺，相傳隋仁壽二年（602）建。清光緒五年（1879）刻本《寧陽縣誌》第173b頁。若真如此，此處稍早應已有佛教團體活動。

div.head: 相關人物
div.p: 6世紀刻銘中僅有一名爲“彭大買”（鳳凰山4.2號）者可辨識，他可能是供養“弥勒佛”佛名（鳳凰山4.1號）的俗家居士。實筆的單字“佛”銘刻（鳳凰山2號）旁邊有三個不可辨的小字殘刻，賴非第73頁。賴非第49－50頁。或許爲另一供養者名。
6世紀相關人物資料匱乏，而10世紀發生在鳳凰山的事變不僅見於此山刻銘，也載於史册。這是一個罕見的案例，因爲山東摩崖銘刻的人名大多文獻未載。該事變涉及兩位著名歷史人物劉鄩（858－921）和張萬進（870－919，賜名張守進），在鳳凰山6號刻銘中均稱爲“太尉” 。二人均爲後梁（907－923）將領，但是張萬進在貞明五年（919）叛變。

刻銘記載劉鄩成功鎮壓此次叛亂，且其軍隊攻克了當地村民吳貴建立的山寨。戚焕麗認爲劉鄩與吳貴聯手戰勝對手張萬進，該觀點爲學者接受。戚焕麗 。周郢 第113－114頁。 周郢第472－473頁。 周郢第46－47頁。 于勇第264－268頁。然而，鳳凰山6號刻銘未證實此觀點。刻銘記載在劉鄩戰勝張萬進後，將士們擒獲吳貴，解救了被集於山寨的村民，所謂“充甑山寨，將吴貴得，合寨老幼小女太（泰）”。而這些將士很可能即是鳳凰山6號刻銘開端列出的該刻銘的作者。

figure.head: 鳳凰山6號和10號刻銘分佈示意圖
Distribution of inscriptions FHS 6 and 10

div.p: 刻銘亦詳載劉鄩獲得的榮銜： 太（泰）寧軍節度押衙、 銀青光禄大夫、 撿校國子祭酒、 上柱國、 察御史大夫。

劉鄩的生平，見於正史，歐陽脩（1007－1072）主修《新五代史》《新五代史》卷三，第27頁及卷二十二，第225－228頁。和司馬光（1019－1086）編纂《資治通鑒》《資治通鑒》卷二百七十，第8834頁及卷二百七十一，第8850－8851頁。提供下述資料：
858年，生於山東某地方將領家。
881年起從軍，900年左右該軍隊投效後梁開國君主朱温（852－912；907－912年在位）。
約自900年起，直屬朱温的軍隊。
907年，協助朱温政變成功，建立新王朝。
913年，封鎮南節度使和開封府尹。
917年，因作戰失利，被貶爲亳州團練使。
918或919年，再度任命爲太尉，奉敕擊退反叛者張太尉。鳳凰山6號所載即爲此功。
921年，自願告老還鄉。時君主疑其不忠，於6月10日將其毒害。
鳳凰山6號紀年爲公元921年10月12日，即劉太尉去世後4個月。鳳凰山6號開始提到的五位及鳳凰山6a號提及的一位地方將領，這六位地方官極有可能爲劉太尉的屬下，試圖以刻銘永存被毒害的太尉之功。因此，山頂的刻銘可視爲緬懷其人之物（）。
鄰近的鳳凰山7號、8號和10號也可能與此事件有關。上文提及劉鄩曾任開封府尹。《新五代史》卷二十二，第226頁。鳳凰山7號提到一位來自開封的劉公，可能指的就是劉鄩。鳳凰山8號殘字同樣涉及軍事征討。
鳳凰山9號提到含有“竧”字的人名。刻銘風化嚴重，上下文不可辨。鳳凰山10號則爲某陳氏於某年九月九日所書，年號損毀難識，可能與鳳凰山6號的時間相同，即書於龍德元年九月九日（公元921年10月12日）。

鳳凰山6號所記事件見於正史。《新五代史·後梁末帝本紀》載：是歲［貞明四年］，泰寧節度使張守進叛附於晋。亳州團練使劉鄩爲兗州安撫制置使以討之 ……［貞明五年］冬十月，劉鄩克兗州，張守進伏誅。《新五代史》卷三，第27頁。
鳳凰山6號提及的張太尉即張守進，《新五代史·劉鄩傳》則作張萬進：兗州張萬進反，拜鄩兗州安撫制置使。萬進敗死，乃拜鄩泰寧軍節度使。《新五代史》 卷二十二，第227頁。
司馬光（1019－1086）《資治通鑒》也作張萬進，記録更詳盡：泰寧節度使張萬進，輕險好亂。時嬖倖用事，多求賂於萬進，萬進聞晋兵將出，己酉，遣使附於晋，且求援。以亳州團練使劉鄩爲兗州安撫制置使，將兵討之。《資治通鑒》卷二百七十，第8834頁。
司馬光還提供了該事件更多的細節：劉鄩圍張萬進於兗州經年，城中危窘。晋王方與梁人戰河上，力不能救。萬進遣親將劉處讓乞師於晋，晋王未之許。處讓於軍門截耳曰：“苟不得請，生不如死！”晋王義之，將爲出兵。會鄩已屠兗州，族萬進，乃止。《資治通鑒》卷二百七十一，第8850－8851頁。
文獻記載同一事件。而細節有别，且文體不同：一以紀念個人的方式刻於僻處石刻（鳳凰山6號）；一是作爲官方記録的正史。鳳凰山6號刻銘突出的是劉鄩的豐功偉績，列舉衆多光鮮的名號。正史則體現教化，着重闡述張太尉如何背叛後梁。

figure.head: 鳳凰山北端刻經石群的東面：1號刻銘位於靠後的大圓石，9號刻銘位於左前方的大圓石。2007年拍攝
East side of the northern group of inscribed boulders; the boulder in the rear carries the inscription FHS 1, the left one in front carries FHS 9; photograph taken in 2007

div.p: 除了功能與目的不同，兩種文獻所記的時間也有所出入。 根據不同文獻，叛變或始於後梁貞明四年（918）七月，或八個月之後的後梁貞明五年（919）三月。 劉鄩戰勝張萬進的時間，或爲後梁貞明五年（919）十月，或將近兩年之後的貞明七年（921）八月。1084年，司馬光已經注意到正史記載的這些差異。《資治通鑒》卷二百七十，第8834頁。中華書局1976年版《舊五代史》的編者也討論過這個問題，認爲應該遵循《舊五代史》的記述。《舊五代史》 第138頁。
于勇2008年迻録並討論的寧陽縣博物館藏張萬進墓誌，于勇。墓誌照片見袁明英第7册：第2257頁。支持事變參與者刊刻的鳳凰山6號刻銘所記時間。因此，時間問題可以重新確立：貞明五年（919）元月，張萬進叛變；同年二月九日（919年3月13日），劉鄩受命平定叛亂。乙卯年十二月四日（919年12月4日），劉鄩完成使命，處決了張萬進。于勇 第263頁和第267頁。

figure.head: 鳳凰山北端刻經石群的南面，6號刻銘位於左邊的大圓石上。2007年拍攝
The northern group of inscribed boulders, seen from the south. The boulder to the left carries the inscription FHS 6; photograph taken in 2007

div.head: 斷代
div.p:
鳳凰山佛名刻銘無紀年。賴非賴非第117頁。和桐谷征一桐谷征一第78頁。根據書法風格認爲這些佛名是僧安道壹（活動於562－579年）的早期作品。鳳凰山未見僧安道壹刻名，兩者之間關聯不明，因而這一看法有待商榷。但 可將鳳凰山1號“大空王佛”與刻於河北省南響堂山的同一佛名（）作比較。 後者字號比前者大兩倍有餘，
馬忠理第51頁提供此佛名的基本信息，稱佛名高達兩米以上。張林堂第95頁提供的測量數據爲40釐米 × 140釐米。但兩者採用相似的筆法及刻法。兩處的“大”“空”及“王”字採用雙鈎法，“佛”字則局部或全部採用單鈎技法。 馬忠理第73頁和張林堂第95頁提供的南響堂山刻銘的拓片顯示，整個“佛”字和“空”字的部分看似單鈎技法，但事實上，僅“佛”字的“人”字旁爲單鈎技法。（） 南響堂山佛名刊刻於6世紀後半葉，與佛名相鄰的像龕有一屬於佛名的563年的題記，但像龕本身與佛名無直接關聯。 與山東地區所刊刻佛名時代相近，鳳凰山1號佛名也應屬於這一時期。
較晚的鳳凰山6號刻銘則有明確紀年，即921年10月12日。鳳凰山7號、8號和10號的紀年無法辨識，但與鳳凰山6號刻銘所記爲同一歷史事件，應當刻於同一時期。

figure.head: 北端與南端刻銘石群之間刊刻鳳凰山2號與3號的大石坪，距鳳凰山1號南面30米左右。2007年拍攝
The large rock surface between the northern and southern group of inscribed boulders carries FHS 2 and FHS 3, about 30 m to the south of FHS 1; photograph taken in 2007

div.head: 研究史
div.p: 直到20世紀90年代，學者纔開始關注鳳凰山刻銘，尤其是該處最長、最清晰的後梁鳳凰山6號刻銘。1994年版《寧陽縣誌》提及六處刻銘，但只介紹鳳凰山6號。編者强調，該刻銘所記事件可與正史互證。《寧陽縣誌》第811頁。 1998年《中國文物報》和1999年《中國歷史學年鑒》將鳳凰山刻銘視爲新發現。《中國文物報》。《中國歷史學年鑒》。 戚焕麗於1999年發表關於鳳凰山6號刻銘的研究，爲目前僅見專文。戚焕麗。她讚揚刻銘書法水平堪與東漢建寧元年（168）的《衡方碑》相比，《衡方碑》，全稱《漢故衛尉卿衡府君之碑》。此碑原立於汶上縣，現存於泰安市博物館。還列舉《新五代史》和《資治通鑒》中與刻銘所記事件及人物相關的段落，並就事件闡釋作出推論。見上文“相關人物”部分和下文鳳凰山6號“討論”部分。但是以上文章均未提及該山佛教刻銘。

周郢援引戚焕麗的文章，强調鳳凰山6號刻銘作爲五代史研究資料的重要性。周郢 第113－114頁。 周郢第472－473頁。 周郢第46－47頁。 他提及中華書局編者對事件時間在正史記載中相矛盾的討論，《舊五代史》 第138頁。認爲石刻時間真實。周郢 第114頁。 2008年，于勇發表寧陽縣博物館藏張萬進墓誌。于勇。爲鳳凰山6號刻銘提到的事件提供新視角，且將該事件置於廣闊的歷史脈絡中討論。張萬進生卒年亦得以確定。

本世紀初，僅發怖一處佛名（弥勒佛；鳳凰山4.1號）、一則人名（鳳凰山4.2號）和一個“佛”字（鳳凰山1號最後一字）。張總第4頁和第41頁，註14。
2003年和2006年先後出版的《北朝摩崖刻經研究》第二和第三卷提及上述銘刻，並列爲僧安道壹的早期作品，未做深入分析。桐谷征一第51、76和78頁。 賴非第117頁。 賴非第13頁。
賴非於2007年發表了鳳凰山1號至5號佛名首次系統記録，包括其測量數據、録文和每字的保存情况。賴非。他分析其書法風格，並指出均爲僧安道壹早期之作。賴非第220、238、240、247、251及253頁。 他2015年出版的近著重申此看法。賴非第12頁和第21頁。迄今未見討論此山佛名刻銘的宗教意義。

div.head: 總目
div.p: 鳳凰山刻銘包括五處佛名（鳳凰山1號、2號、3號、4.1號和5號）和六則題記（鳳凰山4.2號、6號、7號、8號、9號和10號），未見佛經節文。其中三處佛名完整無缺，即最北端的鳳凰山1號及最南端的鳳凰山4.1號和5號，位於山頂中部的鳳凰山2號及3號僅存“佛”字。

“大空王佛”（鳳凰山1號）在山東省境内已發現15處， 多於其他佛名。列表見上文，。
如《中國佛教石經·山東省》前兩卷所述，藏經並無與之完全相同的佛名，此名應爲從事摩崖刻經的僧人所創，可理解爲偉大的空王佛，或尊崇大空的王佛。 前者意爲過去佛──空王佛，詳論見《中國佛教石經·山東省》第一卷第60－63頁。後者意爲“空”的象徵。“空”是刻於山東地區的《般若經》節文最常見的概念。這十五個例子，在數量上遠超過其他佛名刻銘。其他經常出現的佛名“彌勒佛”以及“阿彌陀佛”，各刊刻四次。“彌勒佛”刻銘見於洪頂山9.9號、鳳凰山4.1號、徂徠山9號和崗山1號。“阿彌陀佛”之名則見於洪頂山9.10號、大寨山1號、徂徠山9號和崗山1號。佛祖釋迦牟尼及觀世音佛名號各出現三次。大空王佛在山東顯然非常受到推崇。鳳凰山1號佛名刊刻於較高的位置，前可設座（），極適合作爲禪修之所。
鳳凰山4號爲彌勒佛，此佛名見於洪頂山9.9號和徂徠山9號。即未來佛，於末法後自兜率天而降，與往生於此新世界的眾生共聚一堂。
鳳凰山5號爲華光佛，在北齊（550－577）的疆域内，該佛名僅見於此。《法華經》中，佛陀預言舍利弗在未來成此佛： 舍利弗！汝於未來世，過 無量無邊不可思議劫，供養若干千萬億佛， 奉持正法，具足菩薩所行之道，當得作佛， 號曰華光如來、應供、正遍知、明行足、善逝、世 間解、無上士、調御丈夫、天人師、佛、世尊。《法華經》T#262，9：11b16－20。英譯採自華茲生第51頁，僅佛名稍作修改。
佛陀進而描述華光佛的佛土：國名離垢，其土平正，清净嚴飾，安隱豐樂，天人熾盛。琉璃爲地，有八交道，黄金爲繩以界其側。其傍各有七寶行樹，常有華菓。華光如來亦以三乘教化眾生。《法華經》T#262，9：11b20－24。英譯採自華茲生第52頁，僅末句稍作修改。
包括兩處殘缺佛名（鳳凰山2號和3號）的五處佛名號，將鳳凰山營造成涵蓋無限時間和空間性的一方聖土。大空王佛象徵遥遠而無盡的過去。彌勒佛和華光佛昭示着無窮盡的未來。於此刊刻佛名的僧人修習菩薩道，推崇《法華經》和《般若經》之類的基本佛典，並立誓修身成佛。
6世紀的題記表明，在動蕩的時期，僧人和信眾奉鳳凰山爲佛陀庇護的聖地。彭大買（鳳凰山4.2號）等供養人可能期許在此劫滅後，能往生於彌勒佛主持的净土世界。
鳳凰山未見隋（581－618）、唐（618－907）統一帝國時期的題記。907年，朱温建立後梁，中土四分五裂。如鳳凰山6號至8號及鳳凰山10號刻銘所示，在這個動蕩並延續不超過16年的短命王朝，鳳凰山再次成爲避難所， 不僅用作山寨，更是恢復和平的一個領地。
鳳凰山9號毗鄰鳳凰山1號，兩者可能有關聯。但由於該題記已嚴重風化，其内容不可辨識。

div.head: 刊刻技法
div.p: 由刻字筆畫的横斷面分析，鳳凰山刻經的刊刻技法分爲兩類。 一、“V”形刻法，即從筆劃兩邊斜綫入鏨，雙邊綫相交，自然形成。二、“雙鈎”刻法，即將筆劃雙邊綫單鏨刻出，保留筆劃内部。
“大空王佛”（鳳凰山1號）的前三個字以“雙鈎”刻成，最後一個“佛”字以實心“V”形技法刻成。 這一不同之處或許起因於强調“佛”的概念。山東境内其他14則“大空王佛”刻銘，也有並用實刻和雙鈎兩種技法，但並没有與此完全相符的例子。值得注意的是，雙鈎刻法僅用於這一佛名，其他佛名均未用此技法。有關這些佛名刻法中的飛白，見魯大東和尤麗。

div.head: Mount Fenghuang
div.p: Mount Fenghuang 鳳凰山 is one of several mountains in Shandong where sixth century inscriptions focus on Buddha names. The others are Mount Tao 陶山 in this volume, and Mount Tianchi 天池山, East Mount Shuyuan 書院東山, Mount Ergu 二鼓山, Mount Yuncui 雲翠山, and Mount Dazhai 大寨山 in . The Buddha names conjure up the cosmic space and time of the Buddhist universe.

Yet, the unique features of Mount Fenghuang are several tenth century inscriptions. They are testimony to a rebellion against the ruling emperor, Zhu Zhen 朱瑱 (r. 913–923), often referred to as the Last Emperor (Modi 末帝) of the Latter Liang 後梁 dynasty (907–923), at which time the peak served as a mountain stronghold. The incident is also recorded in official sources, and the inscriptions thus offer us an opportunity to compare history at the grass roots, recorded by and for locals, with official history writing sanctioned by the government.

div.head: Location
div.p: Mount Fenghuang lies at the western end of one of the ridges of Shandong’s central ranges. It is part of Ningyang County 寧陽縣. The peak with the inscription is located 17 km east-northeast of the county seat, 23 km north of Qufu City 曲阜市, and 45 km south-southwest of Tai’an City 泰安市 (map in front endpaper). The geographic position is 116.9716° east and 35.8001° north WGS84. This equals 497430 E (easting) and 3961780 N (northing) on the meridian 50S UTM.

The Mount Fenghuang range has a north-south extension of about 6 km, and an east-west extension of about 8 km. Its highest peak has an altitude of 530 m above sea level, and its foot at the west is at 120 m.

The inscriptions are located on a plateau on the southwestern peak called Great Mountain Peak 大山頂子. About 3 km northeast of Geshi Township 葛石鎮, it rises steeply about 400 m above Liuzhuang Village 劉莊村 deep in the valley to the west. The Great Mountain Peak, seen from above, has a pear-shaped outline (fig. ). The trail leading to the peak is known only in the neighborhood. From the peak, the view extends east toward the main hills of the Fenghuang ranges, and to the south over the plain around Qufu. In the north are further higher ridges. In the southeast is Huangjia Valley 黄家峪.

The neighboring inscription site, Mount Shuiniu 水牛山, lies 28 km to the west-southwest. On a high cliff, the inscription SNS 1 is facing south-southwest and is thus not within the line of sight from the peak of Mount Fenghuang. In the same way, the inscription site on Mount Fenghuang is not visible from Mount Shuiniu. The closest inscription sites to the south would have been those, now destroyed, on Mount Jian 尖山, 40 km away. Mount Jian and Mount Fenghuang are within each other’s line of sight. Mount Jian’s former inscriptions were facing north toward Mount Fenghuang. The inscriptions on Mount Culai are located 45 km to the northeast. Its southeastern mountain range with the inscriptions is not visible from Mount Fenghuang.

figure.head: 鳳凰山和西南方村鎮示意圖。圖片來源：谷歌地球™
Mount Fenghuang and the townships in the southwest; picture provided by Google™Earth

div.head: Topography and Spatial Grouping
div.p:
Great Mountain Peak on Mount Fenghuang culminates in a plateau, oval in shape, 80 m north to south, with a maximum width of 40 m (fig. ). Inscriptions of five Buddha names are strung out along the plateau north to south. The first inscription in the north, FHS 1, is that of Buddha King of Great Emptiness (Da kong wang fo 大空王佛). It faces east (fig. ). The next two Buddha names, about 28 m to the south, are engraved on flat rock, looking up to the sky. However, in both cases only the last character, fo
佛, remains (FHS 2 and FHS 3; fig. ). The name of Buddha Maitreya (Mile fo 彌勒佛; FHS 4.1, with the donor name FHS 4.2), about another 24 m to the south, also faces the sky. Nearby, on a small boulder, which has fallen sideways, the name Buddha Flower Radiance (Padmaprabha; Huaguang fo 華光佛; FHS 5) is engraved on one of its two faces visible in figure .

The longest and most important historical inscription (FHS 6), which has a date of 921, is on a boulder facing south, on the western side of a passageway (fig. ). A further single column, FHS 6a, on the same boulder, but facing east, provides additional information on FHS 6. On a boulder further back, but on the same side of the passageway, is the short inscription, FHS 7, facing southeast. Inscription FHS 8 on a boulder on the opposite side of the passageway, faces south, like FHS 6. Again on the same boulder, but facing east, is FHS 9 (fig. ). Adjacent, to the north of this boulder, is the boulder with FHS 1, already mentioned. FHS 10 on a sloping rock, about 4 m to the south, faces south (fig. ).

figure.head: 鳳凰山南端刻經石群局部，5號刻銘位於前方斷層後的圓石上。2007年拍攝
Detail of the southern group of boulders; FHS 5 is carved on the boulder behind the split rock at the front; photograph taken in 2007

div.head: Place Name
div.p: Mount Fenghuang 鳳凰山, Mount Phoenix, is obviously an auspicious name rooted in a tradition that phoenixes (fenghuang 鳳凰) lingered there.

Other names are recorded, too, but, as one might expect, it is sometimes difficult to assign them to specific locations, and there are disagreements. We will now survey what the local gazetteers have to say about the names given to the mountain and its surroundings.

The name Mount Fenghuang occurs for the first time in the
Gazetteer of Yanzhou Prefecture (Yan­zhou Fu zhi 兗州府誌) with prefaces of 1572 and 1573. It mentions only that “Mount Fenghuang is situated in the northeast of the county 鳳凰山在縣東北”, without giving further details. 2: 134. Yet the same gazetteer talks about another mountain, Weijiazhai Shan 魏家寨山, literally “the Mountain with the holdout of the Wei Clan,” and locates it “30 miles west of the [Ningyang] county seat 在縣西三十里.” 2: 133. The description says:
div.quote:
This mountain is high and steep. Above is a cave in the rock, which can accommodate several hundred people. During the Yuan period [1280–1368], people often fled to this place to avoid warfare. 其山高峻，上有石洞. 可容數百人。元季人多避兵在此。
2: 133.
div.p: The Comprehensive Gazetteer of Shandong of 1533 similarly talks about a “Mountain with the holdout of the Wei Clan 魏家寨山”, but locates it “20 miles west of the Ning­yang county seat 在寧陽縣西二十里.” 1: 349.

The Kangxi 康熙 era (1662–1722) Gazetteer of Ningyang County (Ningyang Xian zhi 寧陽縣誌) of 1702 does not talk about a Mount Fenghuang 鳳凰山 at all, but only about a Fenghuang Ridge (Feng­huang­ling 鳳凰嶺), as the westernmost peak of a range named Nine Peak Mountain (Jiuding Shan 九頂山). 573. This description seems to fit the topography. The summit with the inscription site is, indeed, on the westernmost of a row of nine peaks. This gazetteer also talks about the holdout of the Wei Clan 魏家寨 and describes it in the same terms as the Wanli era Gazetteer of Yanzhou Prefecture. However, it locates this holdout 50 miles east of the county seat. 581.

The Qianlong 乾隆 era (1736–1795) Gazetteer of Ningyang County (Ningyang Xian zhi 寧陽縣誌) of 1743 transfers the stronghold to a mountain, which it names Mount Fenghuang 鳳凰山. It quotes the stronghold’s description from the two earlier gazetteers, but with several changes. The stronghold becomes one for the Ning clan 寧家; there is no longer a cave in the rock, but a flat area, and the number of people, who fled to the top, is enlarged from several hundred to several thousand:
div.quote:
Mount Fenghuang is located 50 miles from the county seat (east of Liugou Village). At the top was a holdout of the Ning clan. The mountain is high, steep, and dangerous in the extreme. A very narrow trail leads up to the top. There, a flat area can accommodate several thousand people. During the Yuan period [1280–1368], people often fled to this place to avoid warfare. 鳳凰山在縣城五十里（柳溝莊東），上有寧家寨。高峻險絶，鳥道而上，有平地可容數千人。元季人多避兵於此。
65.
div.p: The description, “the mountain is high, steep, and dangerous in the extreme. A very narrow trail leads up to the top”, although evocative, is not specific enough; it could apply to many peaks in the area, and the summit with the engravings cannot possibly accommodate a few thousand people. Yet, the Qianlong Gazetteer contains a novel observation about the rocks on the mountain:
div.quote:
The rocky mountain lends itself to being [quarried and] polished. People from far and wide make use of it. 山石可磨，遠近利之。
65.
div.p: The compiler of the Xianfeng 咸豐 era (1851–1874) Gazetteer of Ningyang County (Ningyang Xian zhi 寧陽縣誌) of 1852 was Chen Jixun 陳紀勛 (mid-19th century). He will be met with again in the volume on Mount Tai 泰山 (Shandong, volume 4), as he wrote a colophon in Sutra Stone Valley (Jingshiyu 經石峪) in 1857. His gazetteer is well researched. For Mount Fenghuang, he takes over the main part from the Qianglong gazetteer, but he elaborates on the mountains in the area:
div.quote:
To the east [of Mount Fenghuang] is Mount Rushes (Mao Shan 茅山), adjacent to it is the Great Canyon Cavity (Dayukou 大嵎口); to its south, is, on the left, Mount Sweet Potatoes (Shu Shan 薯山), and, on the right, Mount Wolf (Lang Shan 狼山). In the southeast is Mount Dragon (Long Shan 龍山). 東爲茅山，傍即大嵎口；南左爲薯山，右爲狼山；東南爲龍山。
6b.
div.p: In this topography, it appears that, Mount Wolf, “to the south on the right” is our peak with the inscriptions, that is Great Mountain Peak. This is confirmed by the commentator, Ding Zhao 丁昭, who writes in 2003, in his Compendium with Commentaries of Ming and Qing Dynasty Gazetteers from Ningyang County (Ming Qing Ningyang Xian zhi huishi 明清寧陽縣誌匯釋): “Mount Wolf is located west of Huangjia valley 狼山: 黄家峪西之山” (fig. ). 83, note 15.

Chen Jixun further quotes an unknown edition of the General Gazetteer of Shandong (Shandong tong­zhi 山東通誌) which mentions Mount Steamer (Zeng Shan 甑山). He identifies Mount Steamer as Nine Peak Mountain, which, he says, is also known as Nine Immortals’ Mountain (Jiuxian Shan 九仙山). “九頂山又名九仙山，即甑山也。” 6b。 The character zeng 甑 occurs in the tenth century inscription FHS 6, testifying that the name Mount Steamer was current at the time.

The Guangxu 光緒 era (1875–1908) Gazetteer of Ningyang County (Ningyang Xian zhi 寧陽縣誌) of 1879 does not add new information, but takes over what Chen Jixun had said in 1852. 39a. It is often quoted by modern scholars, who apply all its information to the mountain with the engravings, without considering the difficulties of identification presented here.

The Gazetteer of Ningyang County of 1994 still calls the mountain, Fenghuang, 90. but in 1998, when a forest and geological park was established on the range, this time-honoured poetic name was abolished and replaced by a seemingly more uplifting appellation, Mount Shentong (Shentong Shan 神童山), literally “Mount Child Prodigy”. This mountain includes the peak with the inscription and the surrounding area.

Nevertheless, the Shandong Stone Carving Art Museum (Shandong shike yishu bowuguan 山東石刻藝術博物館) has always retained the traditional name, even in its latest publication of 2015. 71–73; 49–50. Therefore, we also use it in the present volumes.

div.head: Related Monasteries
div.p: The monastery responsible for the inscriptions with Buddha names is unknown. Among the monasteries that are mentioned in gazetteers, Shijie Monastery 石碣寺, originally Yungai Monastery 雲蓋寺, is the oldest and also the closest to the inscription site. According to tradition it was founded in 602, 173b. yet there may have been an earlier Buddhist establishment at the site.

div.head: Related Persons
div.p: In the sixth century engravings, only one personal name, that of a certain Peng Damai 彭大買, can be identified (FHS 4.2). He was probably the lay donor of the Buddha name, Maitreya (FHS 4.1). Next to the single character fo 佛, carved with solid strokes (FHS 2), are three small illegible characters. 73; 49–50. They may be the name of another donor.

While the information for sixth century related persons is thus rather meagre, an incident in the tenth century at Mount Fenghuang is described in several inscriptions there and also in historical sources. This is a rare case. Most individuals who appear in the Shandong cliff inscriptions are not otherwise known. The event in question involved two well-known historical figures; both are called “Great Commander (taiwei 太尉)” in the inscription FHS 6, Liu Xun 劉鄩 (858–921), and Zhang Wanjin 張萬進 (870–919), on whom the emperor once bestowed the name Zhang Shoujin 張守進. Both served as generals under the Latter Liang 後梁 dynasty (907–923), but Zhang Wanjin rebelled in 919.

The inscription talks about Liu Xun’s victory over the rebel, but also about the local villager Wu Gui 吴貴, who built a mountain holdout which was equally conquered by Liu’s troops. Qi Huanli 戚焕麗 has suggested that the triumph of General Liu Xun over his opponent Zhang Wanjin was brought about by his alliance with Wu Gui, and scholars have accepted this view. . 113–114. 472–473. 46–47. 264–268. The inscription FHS 6, however, does not confirm this. It rather makes it clear that after Liu Xun had defeated Zhang Wanjin, some of his commanders caught Wu Gui and saved the villagers that he had kept in the mountain holdout. The inscription says: [We] cleared out the mountain holdout thoroughly and captured Wu Gui. All, the old and young, children and women from the mountain holdout were brought to safety. 充甑山寨，將吴貴得，合寨老幼小女太（泰）。 These commanders were likely those listed at the beginning of FHS 6 as the authors of the inscription.

The inscription also lists all the titles, which Great Commander Liu Xun was bestowed with after his victory:
Military Commissioner of the Tai­ning army 太（泰）寧軍節度押衙
Grand Master of Imperial Entertainments with Silver Seal and Blue Ribbon 銀青光禄大夫
Honorary Chancellor of the National University 撿校國子祭酒
Supreme Pillar of State 上柱國
Grand Master of the Investigating Censorate 察御史大夫.

Two historical works, 3: 27; 22: 225–228; juan 270: 8834; 271: 8850–8851. the New History of the Five Dynasties (Xin Wudai shi
新五代史) by Ouyang Xiu 歐陽脩 (1007–1072), and the Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government (Zizhi tongjian 資治通鑒) by Sima Guang 司馬光 (1019–1086), provide the following information on the life of Great Commander Liu Xun:
In 858, he was born in Shandong into a family of officials.
From 881 onward, he made a career in the militia, which around 900 joined Zhu Wen 朱温 (852–912; r. 907–912), the later founder of the Latter Liang dynasty.
From about 900 onward, Liu Xun served in the army of Zhu Wen.
In 907, he assisted Zhu Wen in his successful coup that led to the founding of the new dynasty.
In 913, he was appointed Military Commissioner of Zhennan 鎮南 and Prefect of Kaifeng (Kaifeng yin 開封尹).
In 917, following defeat in battle, he was demoted to Military Training Commissioner (Tuan­lian shi 團練使) of Bo Prefecture 亳州.
In 918/919 he received a new appointment as commander, with the objective to defeat the rebel Zhang. The inscription FHS 6 reports his success.
In 921, he retired at his own request. The Emperor doubted his loyalty and had him poisoned on June 10.

FHS 6 is dated to October 12, 921, four months after the death of Great Commander Liu Xun. The six local officials, five mentioned at the beginning of FHS 6 and one in FHS 6a, were probably Liu’s former subordinates, and it seems that with their inscription they intended to perpetuate the heroic deeds of their poisoned commander. The engraved rock on the very top of the mountain can thus be taken as a memorial to him (fig. ).

The nearby inscriptions, FHS 7, FHS 8, and FHS 10, probably relate to the same incident. As noted above, Liu Xun was at one time Prefect of Kaifeng. 22: 226. FHS 7 mentions a certain Lord Liu from Kaifeng and may refer to Liu Xun. The surviving characters of FHS 8 point toward a military victory as well.

FHS 9 refers to a person whose name includes the character jing 竧. Yet, owing to the poor condition of the inscription, the context cannot be established. FHS 10 was written by a certain Chen on the ninth day of the ninth month; the characters for the year are damaged and illegible. This year may be the same as that of FHS 6, which was written on the ninth day of the ninth month of 921 (October 12, 921).

The official histories preserve a brief account of the incident. In the “Annals of the Last Emperor of the Latter Liang” (“Hou Liang modi benji” 後梁末帝本紀) in the New History of the Five Dynasties one reads: In 918, the Military Commissioner of Tai­ning, Zhang Shoujin rebelled and placed the prefecture under the Jin. Liu Xun, originally the Military Training Commissioner of Bo Prefecture, was appointed as Military Commissioner to pacify Yan Prefecture, and led troops to defeat him. ... In winter, in the tenth month [919], Liu Xun conquered Yan Prefecture, and Zhang Shoujin was captured and killed. 是歲［貞明四年］，泰寧節度使張守進叛附於晋。亳州團練使劉鄩爲兗州安撫制置使以討之 ……［貞明五年］冬十月，劉鄩克兗州，張守進伏誅。
3: 27. Great Commander Zhang of FHS 6 is identified in this text as Zhang Shoujin 張守進. Yet the “Biography of Liu Xun” (Liu Xun zhuan 劉鄩傳) in the New History of the Five Dynasties names him Zhang Wanjin 張萬進:
div.quote:
Zhang Wanjin of Yan Prefecture rebelled; [Liu] Xun was charged as Military Commissioner to pacify Yan Prefecture. After [Zhang] Wanjin had been defeated and killed, [Liu] Xun was made Military Commissioner of Taining. 兗州張萬進反，拜鄩兗州安撫制置使。萬進敗死，乃拜鄩泰寧軍節度使。
22: 227.
div.p:
In his Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government, Sima Guang also identifies Great Commander Zhang as Zhang Wanjin and gives a more detailed account: [In 918], the Military Commissioner of Tai­ning, Zhang Wanjin, was deceitful and a committed rebel. At the time, the treacherous officials favored [by the enemy] took action, frequently bribing Wanjin. When Wanjin heard that the Jin army was about to set out [to conquer the Liang], on the jiyou day, he sent envoys [offering] to surrender to Jin, as well as to seek their support. Liu Xun, originally the Military Training Commissioner of Bo Prefecture, was then appointed Military Commissioner to pacify Yan Prefecture. He led troops and defeated Zhang Wanjin. 泰寧節度使張萬進，輕險好亂。時嬖倖用事，多求賂於萬進，萬進聞晋兵將出，已酉，遣使附於晋，且求援。以亳州團練使劉鄩爲兗州安撫制置使，將兵討之。
juan 270: 8834.

Sima Guang has even more details of the incident: Over several years, Liu Xun laid siege to Zhang Wanjin at Yan Prefecture; the city was in great danger and in a desperate situation. At that time, the King of Jin was battling the Liang at the [Yellow] River, and thus was not able to offer aid. Wanjin sent his trusted general, Liu Churang 劉處讓, to Jin to plead for military help, but the King of Jin did not meet the request. At the gate to the military headquarters, Churang chopped off his ear and said, ‘If the request is not met, I prefer to die rather than to live!’ The King of Jin, moved by Chu­rang’s uprightness, agreed to deploy troops. However, by that time, [Liu] Xun had already massacred [the people of] Yan Prefecture and killed Wanjin. [The King of Jin] therefore called off [his troops]. 劉鄩圍張萬進於兗州經年，城中危窘，晋王方與梁人戰河上，力不能救。萬進遣親將劉處讓乞師於晋，晋王未之許。處讓於軍門截耳曰：“苟不得請，生不如死！”晋王義之，將爲出兵。會鄩已屠兗州，族萬進，乃止。
271: 8850–8851.

The sources offer different details of the same event. They also represent two different text genres: one is an inscription, engraved in a remote area, in the style of a personal memorial (FHS 6), and the others are the official records in the standard histories. The engraving, FHS 6, concentrates on the heroic deeds of Liu Xun and glorifies his achievements, listing his numerous prestigious titles. The dynastic histories, by contrast, have a didactic intention. They explain how the Great Commander Zhang betrayed his dynasty, the Latter Liang.

Apart from these differing objectives, the sources also have alternative dates for the event. The rebellion began either in the seventh month of 918 or, eight months later, in the third month of 919. When it comes to Liu Xun’s victory over Zhang Wanjin, the dates given are either the tenth month of 919 or, with a gap of almost two years, the eighth month of 921. Already in 1084, Sima Guang noted the discrepancies in the dynastic histories.
juan 270: 8834. In 1976, the editors of the Old History of the Five Dynasties (Jiu Wudai shi 舊五代史) in the Zhonghua Publishing House 中華書局 also discussed the problem and suggested to follow the Old History of the Five Dynasties. 138.

figure.head: The engraving “Buddha King of Great Emptiness” at Southern Mount Xiangtang, Hebei Province; photograph taken in 2007
河北省南響堂山“大空王佛”刻銘。2007年拍攝

div.p: Yet the tomb inscription for Zhang Wanjin in the Ningyang County Museum 寧陽縣博物館, which was transcribed and discused by Yu Yong 于勇 in 2008, . For a picture of the inscription, see 7: 2257. supports the dates in FHS 6, which were engraved by participants in the event. The dates can now be reconstructed: in the first month of 919 Zhang rebelled; on the ninth day of the second month of the year (March 13, 919), Liu Xun received the commission to suppress the rebellion. He completed his mission on the fourth day of the twelfth month of the yimao year (December 4, 919). On this day, Zhang Wanjin was executed. 263, 267.

div.head: Dating
div.p:
The inscriptions with the Buddha names are not dated. On the basis of their calligraphic style, Lai Fei 117.
and Kiriya Seiichi 78.
suggest that they are early works by Seng’an Daoyi 僧安道壹 (fl. 562–579). A connection of Seng’an Daoyi with Mount Feng­huang is not self-evident, as his name is not engraved there, and such attribution deserves more consideration. However, it is possible to compare the engraving of the name, “Da kong wang fo 大空王佛” (FHS 1) with the same name engraved at Southern Mount Xiangtang (Nan Xiangtang Shan 南響堂山; fig. ) in Hebei. Although the characters of the latter are more than twice the size, 51, provides the basic information about the engraving and states that the inscription is more than 2 meters high. 95 gives the measurements as 40 by 140 cm. the two inscriptions share similar brushwork and carving technique. Both engravings combine “double-outline (shuanggou 雙鈎)” technique for the characters da 大, kong 空 and wang 王 with solid carvings of single strokes, partly or completely, in the character fo 佛.In the rubbing illustrated by 73, and 95, the entire character fo and parts of the character kong have been rubbed out as if they had been chiseled as solid strokes. In fact, only the two strokes in the radical ren 人 are chiseled as solid strokes. (fig. ) As much of the carving at Southern Mount Xiangtang was undertaken in the second half of the sixth century,The image niches near the engraved Buddha name carry a colophon associated with them, dated to 563. The niches are not, however, directly linked with the Buddha name. and engravings of the Buddha name in Shandong are of a similar date, we can also attribute FHS 1 to the same period.

The later historical inscription, FHS 6, carries a precise date, of October 12, 921. FHS 7, 8 and 10 have no recognizable dates, but as they relate to the same incident as FHS 6, they must have been carved at about the same time.

div.head: Research History
div.p: Only in the 1990s, did scholars begin to pay attention to the engravings on Mount Fenghuang, especially to FHS 6 of the Latter Liang dynasty, the most visible and longest historical inscription at the site. The Gazetteer of Ningyang County (Ningyang Xian zhi 寧陽縣誌) of 1994 mentions six inscriptions, but only introduces FHS 6. The authors emphasize that the event recorded in this inscription is confirmed in official historical sources. 811. The Weekly of China's Cultural Relics (Zhongguo wenwu bao 中國文物報) of 1998 and the Annual Review of the Study of Chinese History (Zhongguo lishixue nianjian 中國歷史學年鑒) of 1999 treat the inscriptions on Mount Fenghuang as new discoveries. . . Also in 1999, Qi Huanli published the only article so far that focuses on FHS 6. . She praised the high quality of its calligraphy and compared it with that of the Stele of Heng Fang (Heng Fang bei 衡方碑) dated to 168.The complete title reads: Han Dynasty Stele of the Former Chamberlain of the Court for the Palace Garrison, Heir Apparent Heng [Fang 方] (Han gu weiweiqing Heng fu jun zhi bei 漢故衛尉卿衡府君之碑). The original location of the stele was in Wenshang County 汶上縣. Now it is preserved in the Municipal Museum of Tai’an (Tai’an Shi bowuguan 泰安市博物館). She further adduced the pertinent passages from the New History of the Five Dynasties and the Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government on the event and the individuals mentioned in the inscription, and she concluded with an interpretation of the incident.See above, Related Persons, and below FHS 6, Discussion. Yet all these articles did not mention the Buddhist engravings on the mountain.

Quoting Qi Huanli’s article, Zhou Ying 周郢 emphasized the importance of FHS 6 as research material for the history of the Five Dynasties. 113–114. 472–473. 46–47. He mentions the discussion by the editors of the Zhonghua Publishing House 中華書局 on the contradicting dates regarding Zhang Wanjin’s rebellion in the historical sources, 138. and concludes that the stone inscription has the true dates. 114. In 2008, Yu Yong published the tomb inscription for Zhang Wanjin in the Ningyang County Museum. . He thereby offered another perspective on the events mentioned in FHS 6, and placed them into a broader historical context. Zhang Wanjin’s dates could thus be established.

figure.head: 由鳳凰山4號南面巨石群俯視南邊遠景。2007年拍攝
Southern group of boulders; with a view to the south from the position of FHS 4; photograph taken in 2007

div.p: At the beginning of the present century, only one Buddha name (Buddha Maitreya, FHS 4.1), one personal name (FHS 4.2), and one character fo 佛 (the last character of FHS 1) had been published. 4 and 41, note 14. In 2003 and 2006, these were mentioned in the second and third volumes of Research on the Sutras Engraved on Polished Cliffs during the Northern Dynasties (Beichao moya kejing yanjiu 北朝摩崖刻經研究). There they are listed as early works by Seng’an Daoyi. 51, 76, 78. 117. 13. No further analysis was attempted.

In 2007, Lai Fei published the first systematic account of the Buddha names (FHS 1–5), including measurements, a transcription, and the state of preservation of each individual character. . He analyzed their calligraphic style and concluded that they were all written by Seng’an Daoyi during his early career. 220, 238, 240, 247, 251, 253. In his latest publication of 2015, he restates his opinion. 12, 21. To date, the religious implications of the Buddha names on the mountain have not been discussed.

div.head: Inventory
div.p: Five Buddha names (FHS 1, 2, 3, 4.1, 5) and six historical inscriptions (FHS 4.2, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10) are found on Mount Fenghuang, but no sutra passage. Three Buddha names are complete. They are FHS 1 at the northern end, and FHS 4.1 and FHS 5 at the southern end. Of two more carvings, FHS 2 and FHS 3 in the middle part of the summit, only the single character fo 佛 has survived.

The name Buddha King of Great Emptiness (FHS 1) appears more often in Shandong than other Buddha names. Fifteen examples are known.See the list, above, . As mentioned in the previous Shandong volumes, the name is not found in exactly this form in the canon. It is a creation of the monks, who persistently carved texts on rocks under the open sky. It can be understood as Great Buddha King of Emptiness or Buddha King of Great Emptiness. The former reading alludes to Buddha King of Emptiness (Kong wang fo 空王佛), who is a Buddha of the past.For more details, see 82–87. With the latter reading it becomes a symbol of Emptiness, a concept which is met with frequently in the Shandong sutra passages from the prajñāpāramitā literature.

These fifteen examples far surpass in number the engravings of other Buddha names. Buddha names that occur frequently are Buddha Maitreya (Mile fo 彌勒佛) and Buddha Amitābha (Amituo 阿彌陀佛); they are both engraved four times.The name Buddha Maitreya occurs at Mounts Hongding (HDS 9.9), Fenghuang (FHS 4.1), Culai (CLS 9), and Gang (GS D1). The name Buddha Amitābha occurs at Mounts Hongding (HDS 9.10), Dazhai (DZ 1), Culai (CLS 9), and Gang (GS D1). The names of the founder of Buddhism, Buddha Śākyamuni (Shijiamuni 釋迦牟尼), and the almighty Buddha Perceiver of the World’s Sounds (Guanshiyin fo 觀世音佛), each appear three times. Obviously, the Buddha King of Great Emptiness was worshipped intensively in Shandong. On Mount Fenghuang, the name has been placed in an elevated position, and one can sit on the rock in front of it (fig. ). FHS 1 thus is well suited for meditation.

FHS 4 is the name of Buddha Maitreya, the Buddha of the future.This Buddha name is found in Shandong at Mount Hongding (HDS 9.9) and at Mount Culai (CLS 9). At the end of our world he will descend from his Tuṣita Heaven and be united with those who are reborn in the new world.

FHS 5 is the name of Buddha Flower Radiance (Padmaprabha; Huaguang fo 華光佛). In the territory of the Northern Qi dynasty 北齊 (550–577) this name occurs only here. In the Lotus Sutra, the Buddha prophesizes to Śāripūtra that, in a future era, he will be this Buddha: Śāripūtra, in ages to come, after a countless, boundless, inconceivable number of kalpas have passed, you will make offerings to some thousands, ten thousands, millions of Buddhas, and will honor and uphold the correct Law. You will fulfill every aspect of the way of the bodhisattva and will be able to become a Buddha with the name Flower Radiance Thus Come One, worthy of offerings, of right and universal knowledge, perfect clarity and conduct, well gone, understanding the world, unexcelled worthy, trainer of people, teacher of heavenly and human beings, Buddha, World-Honored One. 舍利弗！汝於未來世，過無量無邊不可思議劫，供養若干千萬億佛，奉持正法，具足菩薩所行之道，當得作佛，號曰華光如來、應供、正遍知、明行足、善逝、世間解、無上士、調御丈夫、天人師、佛、世尊。 T#262, 9: 11b16–20. English translation by 51, with the exception of the modification in the Buddha name.

The Buddha then goes on to describe the land of Buddha Flower Radiance: Your realm will be called Free from Stain, the land will be level and smooth, pure and beautifully adorned, peaceful, bountiful and happy. Heavenly and human beings will flourish there. The ground will be of lapis lazuli, roads will crisscross it in eight directions, and ropes of gold will mark their boundaries. Beside each road will grow rows of seven-jeweled trees which will constantly flower and bear fruit. And this Flower Radiance Thus Come One will also employ the three vehicles to teach and convert living beings. 國名離垢，其土平正，清净嚴飾，安隱豐樂，天人熾盛。琉璃爲地，有八交道，黄金爲繩以界其側。其傍各有七寶行樹，常有華菓。華光如來亦以三乘教化眾生。 T#262, 9: 11b20–24. English translation after 52, with slight modification in the last sentence.

The five Buddha names, including the fragmentary ones (FHS 2 and FHS 3), create a sacred realm on Mount Fenghuang. It encompasses cosmic time and space. Buddha King of Great Emptiness represents a remote past and even timelessness. Buddha Maitreya as well as Buddha Flower Radiance point to the distant future. The monks, who engraved these Buddha names here, practiced the bodhisattva path, venerated the Buddhist core texts, the Lotus Sutra and the
prajñāpāramitā literature, and they abided by their vows to become Buddhas themselves.

The sixth century inscriptions reveal that, in unsettled times, Mount Fenghuang had become an enclave for monks and laymen protected by the Buddhas. Donors, such as Peng Damai (FHS 4.2), could hope that, after the end of this world, they would be reborn in the new world of Buddha Maitreya.

No inscriptions from the time of the unified empire under the Sui (581–618) and Tang (618–907) dynasties are found on the mountain. In 907, General Zhu Wen founded the Latter Liang dynasty, and China broke up again into small kingdoms. Under this turbulent, short-lived dynasty of not more than 16 years, the mountain became once more a refuge, as witnessed by inscriptions FHS 6–8 and FHS 10. Mount Fenghuang was not only used as a stronghold, but it was also central to the restoration of peace.
Inscription FHS 9 may have been related to FHS 1, as it is placed next to it. Owing to its bad condition, it is not possible to say anything meaningful about it.

div.head: Carving Techniques
div.p: Having examined the carving of the inscriptions through a transverse section analysis of its strokes, we conclude that the techniques employed fall into two categories: 1. V-shaped carving: the carver chisels slanting lines from both sides of a stroke, so that the lines intersect and the stroke naturally takes shape. 2. Double-outline carving: the carver chisels only the outline on either sides of a stroke, so that the interior of the stroke remains untouched.

The first three characters of the name, Da kong wang fo (FHS 1), are written in double outline technique; the last character, fo 佛, is written with solid V-shaped strokes. It is tempting to find a possible reason for this difference in the substantial status of the concept fo. Among the other fourteen engravings of the name in Shandong, one also finds a combination of solid and double outline characters. Yet no example exactly matches this one. Nevertheless, it is remarkable, that double outlines were used only in this name, and never in names of other Buddhas.For a discussion of the Flying White technique in the engravings of these names, see .
